did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning models, specifically Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) including MobileNetV2 and Xception architectures, and Vision Transformers (ViT), to perform binary classification of skin tumors (melanoma vs. non-melanoma and benign vs. malignant) on dermatological images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled as an 'Original article' (Оригинальные публикации) in the journal header. It presents original experimental results from a comparative analysis of CNN and ViT models on independent datasets (3000 and 4800 images), reporting specific performance metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity) and new findings regarding model efficiency in skin tumor diagnostics.

title: Convolutional neural networks and transformers in skin tumor diagnostics: a comparative analysis of the efficiency of artificial intelligence models in computer vision programs
brief_summary: This study compared the efficiency of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and Vision Transformers (ViT) for binary classification of skin tumors, specifically distinguishing melanoma from non-melanoma and benign from malignant lesions. The models, including MobileNetV2, Xception, and ViT architectures, were evaluated on independent datasets comprising 3000 and 4800 dermatological images using accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity metrics. The results demonstrated that ViT outperformed CNNs, achieving an accuracy of 92.93% in the melanoma task and 91.35% in the benign/malignant task, with specificity reaching up to 95%. While ViT provided higher accuracy by analyzing global patterns, it required careful tuning and high-quality data, whereas CNNs remained stable with limited data. The authors conclude that combining both architectures is recommended for clinical use to enhance diagnostic reliability.

lesion_types_studied.details: Used as a positive class in the binary classification task 'melanoma / not melanoma'. Also mentioned in the context of improving diagnostic accuracy and reducing false positives.

Used as the negative class in the binary classification task 'melanoma / not melanoma'. This class was composed of 50% benign melanocytic skin tumors and 50% other benign tumors to balance the dataset.

Used as a positive class in the binary classification task 'benign/malignant'. In the 'melanoma/not melanoma' task, benign melanocytic tumors (50%) and other benign tumors (50%) formed the 'not melanoma' class. The dataset included 45k images from HAM10000 and 380 verified cases.
Used as a positive class in the binary classification task 'benign/malignant'. The study aimed to differentiate these from benign tumors.

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnostic categories: 'melanoma / not melanoma' and 'benign / malignant'.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the objective is to compare CNN and ViT in 'binary classification' tasks. The models predict discrete classes (melanoma vs non-melanoma; benign vs malignant) using architectures like MobileNetV2, Xception, and ViT. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, AUC ROC, and F1-score, which are standard metrics for classification, not regression.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A public dataset of dermatoscopic images of pigmented skin lesions, used as the primary source for the training data in the CNN model.

use.training: Used as the source for the expanded training dataset (increased to 45k images) for the CNN model.

datasets_used.name: Prof. I.A. Lamotkin's Database
datasets_used.description: A private/clinical dataset containing histologically verified cases, used to supplement the training data.

use.training: Used as part of the expanded training dataset (380 verified cases) for the CNN model.

datasets_used.name: Independent Testing Dataset 1
datasets_used.description: An independent set of dermatological images used for testing the models, excluded from training and validation data.

use.testing: Used for testing the binary classification task 'melanoma / not melanoma'.

datasets_used.name: Independent Testing Dataset 2
datasets_used.description: An independent set of dermatological images used for testing the models, distinct from the first testing set.

use.testing: Used for testing the binary classification task 'benign / malignant'.

datasets_used.name: ImageNet21k
datasets_used.description: A large-scale public dataset used for pre-training the Vision Transformer (ViT) model.

use.training: Used for pre-training the Google ViT model encoder to provide high generalization capability.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate and compare the classification performance of CNN and ViT models by analyzing true positives, true negatives, false positives, and false negatives.
To measure the overall proportion of correct predictions for the binary classification tasks.
To measure the proportion of actual positives correctly identified, critical for minimizing classification errors in melanoma diagnosis.
To measure the proportion of actual negatives correctly identified, with ViT demonstrating superiority (up to 95%).

statistical_methods_used.method: AUC ROC (Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To assess the diagnostic ability of the models across different threshold settings.
To evaluate the balance between precision and recall, particularly for malignant neoplasms.
To ensure equal representation of classes during training to prevent systematic classification errors and improve model stability.
To prevent overfitting by halting training when validation accuracy ceased to improve (after 2-3 epochs without improvement).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It compared model performance using descriptive metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, AUC ROC, F1-score) and error matrices without statistical significance testing.

Independent Testing Dataset 1 (Melanoma.metric: Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity, AUC ROC, F1-score
Independent Testing Dataset 1 (Melanoma.notes: ViT showed superiority over CNNs. Xception outperformed MobileNetV2. ViT required 15-20% more data than CNNs. Error matrix counts for ViT: True Negatives 1387, False Positives 113, False Negatives 99, True Positives 1401.

Independent Testing Dataset 2 (Benign vs.metric: Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity, AUC ROC, F1-score
Independent Testing Dataset 2 (Benign vs.notes: ViT accuracy was 91.4% vs 85% for Xception. ViT demonstrated better specificity (up to 95%). Error matrix counts for CNN v1: True Negatives 1888, False Positives 512, False Negatives 1012, True Positives 1359. Error matrix counts for CNN v2: True Negatives 2005, False Positives 395, False Negatives 1317, True Positives 1337.

Training Data Composition.details: Training set expanded to 45,000 images from HAM10000 and 380 verified cases. Class balancing was applied: 'Not Melanoma' class composed of 50% benign melanocytic tumors and 50% other benign tumors. Equal proportions of subtypes within benign/malignant categories for the second task.

architecture_used.main_model: Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) including MobileNetV2 and Xception architectures, and Vision Transformers (ViT). Specifically, a Google ViT model was used.
architecture_used.pretraining: The Google ViT model was pre-trained on the ImageNet21k dataset. CNN models (MobileNetV2 and Xception) utilized transfer learning, with Xception being the primary focus for the second version of the CNN model.
architecture_used.input_size: Not explicitly reported as a single dimension (e.g., 224x224), but ViT processes images as a sequence of fixed-size patches of 16x16 pixels.

architecture_details.MobileNetV2: CNN architecture used as the first version of the CNN model.
architecture_details.Xception: CNN architecture used as the second version of the CNN model. It includes approximately 71 convolutional layers, 36 depthwise separable blocks (including entry, middle, and exit flows), and external skip connections. An extended augmentation pipeline was implemented for this version.
architecture_details.ViT: Vision Transformer architecture. It divides images into a sequence of 16x16 pixel patches which are converted into embeddings and processed by transformer blocks with self-attention mechanisms. The configuration involved partial freezing of the first seven blocks, tuning of the last four blocks and the classifier, and the use of attention maps for interpretability.

training_details.optimizer: Not explicitly named, but hyperparameter optimization was applied.
training_details.augmentation: Horizontal reflections, brightness/contrast correction, CLAHE (Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization), random horizontal shifts (±15%), vertical flips, RGB noise addition (σ=0.1), and saturation changes (±20%).
training_details.regularization: Early stopping was used (after 2-3 epochs without validation accuracy improvement). Class balancing was applied to the training data.
training_details.data: Training data consisted of 45,000 images from HAM10000 and 380 verified cases from Prof. I.A. Lamotkin's Database.

architecture_used.classification_or_output_head: The ViT classification head was replaced and adapted for binary classification tasks ('melanoma/not melanoma' and 'benign/malignant'). CNN models used Softmax with two output neurons for binary classification.

preprocessing_techniques: Data cleaning from artifacts (removal of images with black borders)
Class balancing (forming the 'not melanoma' class from 50% benign melanocytic tumors and 50% other benign tumors; equal proportions of subtypes within benign/malignant categories)
Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization (CLAHE) for contrast enhancement in conditions of shading or overexposure
Geometric transformations: random horizontal shifts (±15% of image width) and vertical flips
Color distortions: addition of RGB noise (σ=0.1) and saturation changes (±20%)
Image patching (splitting images into fixed-size patches of 16x16 pixels for ViT)
Normalization (mentioned as part of the Xception preprocessing pipeline and ViT architecture)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied as part of the extended augmentation pipeline for the Xception model version.
Applied to enhance image quality and handle shading or overexposure.
Used to enhance contrast in conditions of shading or overexposure.
Shifts of ±15% of the image width were applied to simulate variations in skin position during imaging.
Applied as a geometric transformation to simulate variations in skin position.
Noise with a standard deviation (σ) of 0.1 was added to RGB channels to increase robustness to camera artifacts.
Saturation was altered by ±20% to improve robustness to camera artifacts.

Applied during dataset construction: the 'not melanoma' class was formed from 50% benign melanocytic skin tumors and 50% other benign tumors; equal proportions of subtypes were used within benign/malignant categories for the second task.

conclusions: Vision Transformers (ViT) demonstrated superior performance compared to Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) in binary classification tasks for skin tumors.
In the 'melanoma/not melanoma' task, ViT achieved an accuracy of 92.93%, outperforming the Xception CNN model which achieved 88% accuracy.
In the 'benign/malignant' task, ViT achieved an accuracy of 91.35%, outperforming the Xception CNN model which achieved 85% accuracy.
ViT models demonstrated better specificity, reaching up to 95%, which helps reduce false positive results in melanoma diagnostics.
The superior performance of ViT is attributed to its ability to analyze global patterns, such as asymmetry and pigment distribution.
ViT models require careful tuning and high-quality data, and they needed 15-20% more data compared to CNNs.
CNNs (specifically Xception and MobileNetV2) remain a stable solution for scenarios with limited data.
Image artifacts, such as black borders and circles, negatively impacted the accuracy of ViT models, necessitating data cleaning.
The authors recommend combining both CNN and ViT architectures (hybrid solutions) for clinical use to enhance diagnostic reliability and interpretability.
important_remarks: The study is one of the first to directly compare CNN and ViT architectures on clinically relevant metrics for skin tumor classification.
ViT demonstrated superior accuracy (92.93% for melanoma/non-melanoma and 91.35% for benign/malignant) and specificity (up to 95%) compared to CNNs (Xception: 88% and 85% respectively).
ViT models require careful tuning, high-quality data, and 15-20% more data than CNNs to achieve optimal performance.
CNNs (MobileNetV2 and Xception) remain a stable solution for scenarios with limited data.
ViT models are sensitive to image artifacts (e.g., black borders, circles), necessitating rigorous data cleaning.
The authors recommend combining CNN and ViT architectures (hybrid solutions) for clinical use to enhance diagnostic reliability and interpretability.
The study highlights the critical importance of data quality, preprocessing, and class balancing for implementing AI in dermatology.
ViT's superior performance is attributed to its ability to analyze global patterns such as asymmetry and pigment distribution.
